steps.prompt: Which word is the main verb?

hints.mortua: 'Mortua' is a participle here, forming part of a compound verb with 'est'

steps.reveal: mortua = having died (perf. part., nom. fem. sg.)

hints.present active participle: A present active participle would end in -ns/-ntis; 'mortua' is a perfect participle
hints.adjective: While it can function as an adjective, 'mortua' is grammatically a perfect participle of 'morior'
hints.future participle: A future participle would be 'moritura' — 'mortua' is the perfect participle

steps.prompt: The infinitive of 'mortua est' translates to?

hints.to kill: That would be 'occidere/necare' — 'morior' is deponent and means 'to die'
hints.to be dead: Close, but the infinitive 'mori' means 'to die' (the action), not the state

steps.reveal: mortua est = she has died / she died

hints.present indicative: 'Mortua est' with the participle + 'est' forms the perfect of the deponent verb
hints.pluperfect indicative: Pluperfect would be 'mortua erat' — 'mortua est' is perfect
hints.imperfect indicative: Imperfect of a deponent doesn't use the participle form this way

These are noun forms of 'mors' (death), not the verb 'morior'
hints.moror, morari, moratus sum, —: These are the parts of 'morari' (to delay), not 'morior'
hints.moveo, movere, movi, motum: These are the parts of 'movere' (to move)

hints.active: 'Morior' has passive forms but active meaning — that makes it deponent
hints.passive: 'Morior' looks passive but means 'to die' actively — it is deponent
hints.semi-deponent: Semi-deponent verbs are active in present system and deponent in perfect; 'morior' is fully deponent

hints.vocative: Vocative of 'filia' would also be 'filia', but here she is the subject, not being addressed
hints.ablative: Ablative would be 'filia' in form but context shows it is the subject — nominative
hints.accusative: Accusative would be 'filiam' — 'Filia' is nominative subject

hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'filiae' ends in -ae
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'filiae' ends in -ae
hints.5th declension: 5th declension has genitive in -ei — 'filiae' ends in -ae
